text: The surgical setup is modified for the laparoscopic cholecystectomy in a patient with situs inversus. The patient is positioned with legs apart, the surgeon stands between the legs, the camera holder is on the right side, and the assistant is on the left side. The monitor is positioned towards the patient's left shoulder, and a diagram illustrates the modified port positions.

Upon entering the abdomen, the altered anatomy is visible with the liver located on the left side, the fundus of the stomach on the right, and the pylorus on the left. The surgeon uses a 5mm epigastric port to ascertain the position of the gallbladder.

The surgeon places additional working ports below the left costal margin, positioned in the mid-clavicular line and anterior axillary line, to facilitate access to the gallbladder in the patient with situs inversus.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis to carefully remove significant pericholecystic adhesions, exposing the gallbladder amidst inflamed tissue. The altered anatomy due to situs inversus adds complexity to the procedure.
The surgical team uses five millimeter ports for the laparoscopic cholecystectomy, providing flexibility to convert any port to a ten millimeter size for clipping the cystic duct and artery.

The surgeon uses the mid clavicular port as the main working port, which will be converted into a ten millimeter port for clipping the cystic artery and duct. The duodenum is visibly adherent to the Hartmann's pouch of the gallbladder during adhesiolysis.

The surgeon uses a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques to gradually separate the duodenum from the gallbladder, exposing the Calot's triangle. A posterior dissection is performed to enhance the exposure of the Calot's triangle.

The surgeon uses hook diathermy to carefully take down flimsy adhesions, revealing the duodenum dissected off the Hartmann's pouch of the gallbladder, thereby exposing the Calot's triangle structures.

The surgeon performs a meticulous sharp and blunt dissection of the Calot's triangle to expose the cystic duct and cystic artery, utilizing a suction cannula for blunt dissection in the presence of acute inflammation and severe edema.
The surgeon encounters a spurting vessel during dissection, which is controlled using monopolar or bipolar diathermy, visibly stopping the bleeding.
The surgeon creates a window in the posterior Calot's triangle through careful dissection, further defining the posterior window for better visualization of the cystic duct and artery.
The surgeon identifies and dissects the cystic artery, converting the five millimeter mid-clavicular port into a ten millimeter one for better access. The cystic artery is then clipped and cut.

The surgeon performs further dissection to achieve the critical view of safety, milking the cystic duct to move any stones upwards. The surgical field shows the cystic duct clearly, with no stones visible.
The surgeon addresses the wide cystic duct by applying staggered clips to secure it, demonstrating the chosen method for managing the duct's size.
Staggered clips are placed on the wide cystic duct, followed by its transection. The gallbladder is then dissected off the liver using diathermy, with visible cauterization of the tissue.
The surgeon retrieves a single stone that has fallen using a scoop instrument, carefully removing it from the surgical field.

The surgeon thoroughly washes the surgical area with normal saline to remove any spilled bile, clots, and blood after the gallbladder is dissected off the liver. The clips on the cystic duct and artery are checked for secure placement.
The surgeon introduces a specimen retrieval bag and places the gallbladder inside it for extraction. A drain is then placed in the subhepatic space, marking the completion of the surgery.
